source_paper_title: Hidden Convexity, Optimization, and Algorithms on Rotation Matrices

context:
Page 29, Section 8 (Summary and open questions), paragraph 'Convex coordinate projections'

Conjecture 1. The problem CONVEX is NP-hard.
title: Prove NP-hardness of deciding convexity of coordinate projections of rotation matrices
background_latex:
Let \(n\ge 3\). Let \(\mathrm{SO}(n)=\{X\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}:X^\top X=I,\ \det(X)=1\}\) denote the special orthogonal group (rotation matrices).

For a set of matrix coordinates \(S\subseteq [n]\times [n]\) (where \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\)), define the coordinate projection map \(\pi_S:\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\to\mathbb{R}^{|S|}\) by
\[
\pi_S(X) := (X_{ij})_{(i,j)\in S},
\]
i.e., \(\pi_S(X)\) records the entries of \(X\) indexed by \(S\) (in some fixed order).

Define the decision problem \(\mathrm{CONVEX}\): the input is \(n\) and a coordinate set \(S\subseteq [n]\times[n]\); the output is \(\mathrm{TRUE}\) if the image set \(\pi_S(\mathrm{SO}(n))\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{|S|}\) is convex, and \(\mathrm{FALSE}\) otherwise.

Convexity here is in the usual Euclidean sense: a set \(K\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\) is convex if for all \(x,y\in K\) and all \(\lambda\in[0,1]\), \(\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y\in K\).
problem_definition_latex: Determine the computational complexity of \(\mathrm{CONVEX}\). In particular, prove (or refute) that \(\mathrm{CONVEX}\) is NP-hard: given \(n\) and \(S\subseteq [n]\times[n]\), decide whether \(\pi_S(\mathrm{SO}(n))\) is convex.
latex:
Let \(n\ge 3\). Let \(\mathrm{SO}(n)=\{X\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}:X^\top X=I,\ \det(X)=1\}\) denote the special orthogonal group (rotation matrices).

For a set of matrix coordinates \(S\subseteq [n]\times [n]\) (where \([n]=\{1,\dots,n\}\)), define the coordinate projection map \(\pi_S:\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}\to\mathbb{R}^{|S|}\) by
\[
\pi_S(X) := (X_{ij})_{(i,j)\in S},
\]
i.e., \(\pi_S(X)\) records the entries of \(X\) indexed by \(S\) (in some fixed order).

Define the decision problem \(\mathrm{CONVEX}\): the input is \(n\) and a coordinate set \(S\subseteq [n]\times[n]\); the output is \(\mathrm{TRUE}\) if the image set \(\pi_S(\mathrm{SO}(n))\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{|S|}\) is convex, and \(\mathrm{FALSE}\) otherwise.

Convexity here is in the usual Euclidean sense: a set \(K\subseteq\mathbb{R}^d\) is convex if for all \(x,y\in K\) and all \(\lambda\in[0,1]\), \(\lambda x+(1-\lambda)y\in K\).

Determine the computational complexity of \(\mathrm{CONVEX}\). In particular, prove (or refute) that \(\mathrm{CONVEX}\) is NP-hard: given \(n\) and \(S\subseteq [n]\times[n]\), decide whether \(\pi_S(\mathrm{SO}(n))\) is convex.
location_in_paper: Page 29, Section 8 (Summary and open questions), paragraph 'Convex coordinate projections'
open_problem_quote: Conjecture 1. The problem CONVEX is NP-hard.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: NP-hardness is a complexity-theoretic statement typically requiring a reduction, not a finite counterexample search. While small-n experiments might suggest patterns, they cannot certify or refute NP-hardness in general.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could search for reductions from known NP-hard problems to structured instances of coordinate sets \(S\), with automated verification on bounded sizes as a heuristic fitness signal. However, the true objective (a correct polynomial-time reduction) is hard to validate automatically and ultimately needs a human proof.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Ramachandran--Shu--Wang, 2024) formulates CONVEX: given a coordinate pattern \(S\subseteq[n]\times[n]\), decide whether the coordinate projection \(\pi_S(\mathrm{SO}(n))\) is convex. It establishes several sharp positive and negative results that delimit the landscape: every 2-dimensional linear image of \(\mathrm{SO}(n)\) is convex (via a topological connectedness argument using \(\pi_1(\mathrm{SO}(n))\cong\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z}\)), the projection onto strictly upper-triangular coordinates is convex, and these are maximal in the sense that there exist 3-dimensional linear images that are nonconvex and any linear map of rank \(>\binom{n}{2}\) yields a nonconvex image (by Sard-type dimension arguments). These results show that convexity depends delicately on the combinatorics of \(S\), beyond simple dimension counting.

At present there is no forward-citation evidence of a complexity-theoretic resolution, and the problem remains open. The paper itself notes a nearby NP-hard pattern problem: deciding whether \(S\) can be permuted (rows/columns independently, and possibly transposed) into an upper-triangular pattern is NP-hard (cf. Fertin--Rusu--Vialette, 2015). This suggests a plausible reduction route: encode such permutation-pattern constraints into convexity/nonconvexity of \(\pi_S(\mathrm{SO}(n))\), leveraging the fact that upper-triangular-type patterns yield convex images (strictly upper triangular) while certain small augmentations can force nonconvexity (e.g., by creating a 3D image containing a circular slice). Structural obstructions from Fiedler (2009) about submatrices of orthogonal matrices (e.g., rectangles with \(a+b>n\)) provide additional necessary nonconvexity triggers that could serve as gadgets.

A complementary viewpoint comes from the semidefinite description of \(\mathrm{conv}(\mathrm{SO}(n))\) (Saunderson--Parrilo--Willsky, 2015), which realizes \(\mathrm{SO}(n)\) as a quadratic image of a sphere via the spin representation. This connects CONVEX to quadratic convexity questions on spheres and may offer a pathway to reductions by representing coordinate projections as collections of quadratic forms. However, no known results currently translate this into an NP-hardness proof for deciding convexity of \(\pi_S(\mathrm{SO}(n))\), and even membership of CONVEX in NP is unclear due to the lack of succinct certificates of convexity.

sources.title: Hidden convexity, optimization, and algorithms on rotation matrices
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the decision problem CONVEX for coordinate projections \(\pi_S(\mathrm{SO}(n))\) and conjectures NP-hardness; proves several maximal convexity results: all 2D linear images are convex, the strictly upper-triangular coordinate projection is convex, and any linear map of rank \(>\binom{n}{2}\) yields a nonconvex image.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the NP-hardness question and providing structural obstructions (e.g., nonconvexity for some 3D images and for rank \(>\binom{n}{2}\)) that frame what an NP-hardness reduction would need to exploit.

sources.title: Obtaining a triangular matrix by independent row-column permutations
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies the computational complexity of deciding whether a 0-1 pattern can be made triangular by independent row/column permutations; establishes NP-hardness for related pattern-recognition/permutation problems.
sources.connection: The source paper notes that deciding whether a coordinate set \(S\) is (up to permutations/transposition) a subset of the upper-triangular pattern is NP-hard; such pattern NP-hardness may serve as a gadget in reductions for CONVEX.

sources.title: Suborthogonality and orthocentricity of matrices
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives structural constraints on submatrices of orthogonal matrices; in particular, if \(R\) is an \(a\times b\) submatrix of \(U\in O(n)\) with \(a+b>n\), then \(\|R\|_{op}=1\).
sources.connection: Provides a necessary obstruction for convexity of coordinate projections: if \(S\) contains an \(a\times b\) rectangle with \(a+b>n\), then \(\pi_S(\mathrm{SO}(n))\) cannot be convex (as used in the source paper).

sources.title: Doubly stochastic matrices and the diagonal of a rotation matrix
sources.theorem_or_result: Characterizes \(\mathrm{diag}(\mathrm{SO}(n))\) as the parity polytope \(\mathrm{PP}_n\), hence a polyhedral convex set.
sources.connection: A canonical example of a convex coordinate projection of \(\mathrm{SO}(n)\); helps delineate which coordinate patterns yield convex images and suggests that convexity can be subtle and pattern-dependent.

sources.title: Semidefinite descriptions of the convex hull of rotation matrices
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides an LMI (spectrahedral) description of \(\mathrm{conv}(\mathrm{SO}(n))\) via the spin representation; shows exponential size is needed for equivariant representations and connects to topology of \(\mathrm{SO}(n)\).
sources.connection: Underpins the convex-relaxation viewpoint in the source paper and supplies the spin/quadratic-map framework that may be useful for encoding combinatorial hardness into coordinate projections.
